upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very stable, nearly isothermal surface layer with a strong subsidence inversion: surface temp 11.2°C jumps to 15.4°C at 850mb (1524m), indicating a pronounced inversion lid near 1500m MSL. The lapse rate from surface to 850mb is only -2.76°C/km — far below dry adiabatic (9.8°C/km) — meaning thermals are heavily capped and will struggle to exceed 400–500m AGL. Winds veer and increase dramatically with height: calm at surface, 20–21 kts from 300° at 850mb, and 34 kts from 285° at 700mb (3146m), indicating significant wind shear aloft that pilots near or above ridge height at 1750ft should be aware of.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area/SF region. KHAF reporting mist (BR) with visibility 3–6SM and calm winds through 1415Z, indicating marine layer/fog at Half Moon Bay — coastal clearing may be delayed. KSFO calm to light wind, 10SM visibility. KRHV calm, clear — inland valley benign. All stations show calm surface winds as of morning obs, with light pressure gradient.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland thermal/ridge site, NW-facing launch at summit
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's 1750ft launch works primarily on thermal lift from the sun-heated valley floor, with afternoon NW sea breeze providing ridge lift as it funnels through Calaveras Pass. Ideal winds are 8–15 kts from the NW/WNW. The boundary layer needs to exceed ~300m AGL for usable thermals to reach launch.
sites.todaySetup: Today's key limiting factor is the strong low-level inversion sitting near 1500m MSL, which caps thermal development severely — BL heights model at only 400–480m, barely topping the launch elevation. Morning winds at launch are northerly 8 kts, cycling to light and variable (~5–6 kts) by early afternoon as the valley breeze stalls before any sea breeze fills in. The encouraging news: no precipitation, CAPE is zero, and winds are not strong enough to be dangerous. The 1750ft site briefly sees usable NW flow 10–14 kts at 80m level in the morning, but by midday that drops to 6–9 kts at 80m. Afternoon cloud cover builds to 100% by 1500–1700 local (models show overcast), which will kill thermal development entirely. Tomorrow.io shows gusts reaching 15 kts at 1700 with temps rising, but direction shifts to N/NNE — not ideal for this NW-facing site. Winds at 120m (roughly launch height advantage) remain weak 3–6 kts throughout. The thermal window, if it exists at all, is narrow and capped.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Northerly 8–9 kts at surface, 10–12 kts at 80m — on the face this may produce some ridge effect but BL only 175–435m, thermals barely forming. Winds backing toward N, conditions tight for 1750ft launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind lightens to 6–8 kts, backing to near-northerly then NNW by 1300. BL peaks near 480m. Modest thermals possible in a brief window around 1100–1300 before cloud cover encroaches; gusts remain low (~10 kts Tomorrow.io at 1400).

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Cloud cover rises to 100% by 1500 (HRRR/GFS agree), killing thermals. Wind holds 6–7 kts from NW/WNW — marginal ridge lift possible but sky cover makes this a poor flying environment. Not recommended.

verdict.hazards: Strong inversion caps thermals at ~480m AGL; afternoon 100% overcast kills lift; wind shear aloft above 850mb; afternoon gusts to 15 kts (Tomorrow.io) punchy for P2

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft training hill at Ed Levin is a thermal/sledder site best used when the valley is producing light thermals and wind is light NW. It's a short-flight site with a quick turnaround; ideal for P2 practice in 5–12 kt NW/W conditions with no strong gusts.
sites.todaySetup: The 600ft site faces the same inversion problem as the summit today: BL heights of 335–510m AGL are just barely workable for short thermal hops, but morning winds are northerly and light (4–6 kts surface). The key concern for this launch is the Tomorrow.io data showing afternoon gusts reaching 11.9 kts at 1700 with mean winds only 2–3 kts — that significant gust-to-mean ratio signals punchy, thermic air arriving ahead of the sea breeze. The low-level flow is too variable in direction (backing from NE to NW through the day) to consistently face the launch. Afternoon cloud cover goes 100% by 1500 on models. The morning window (before 1200) offers the most benign conditions with light variable flow, but it's essentially a sled-ride situation.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light northerly 4–6 kts, calm gusts. BL building slowly to 370m. Suitable for gentle sled rides and ground handling; thermals very weak.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind lightens further to 3–5 kts, cycling NW to W. BL at 455–510m provides short thermal hops. Cloud increasing 13–27%. Benign but unexciting flying.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Gusts spike to 10–12 kts by 1700 (Tomorrow.io) while mean winds stay 2–3 kts — punchy thermic conditions not suitable for P2. 100% cloud cover arrives by 1500.

verdict.hazards: Gusty-to-mean ratio punchy by afternoon (gusts to 12 kts vs 2–3 kt mean); 100% overcast by 1500; variable wind direction not ideal for launch face

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 300ft bunny hill is a pure training/sledder site. Works best with light W/NW winds 5–10 kts. Used for P1/P2 training flights and ground handling; no soaring expected.
sites.todaySetup: The 300ft site has the most sheltered and mellow conditions of the three Ed Levin launches today. Morning winds are very light (2–3 kts W/NW), building gently through midday as the valley warms. BL heights of 285–405m AGL are more than sufficient for this low launch. The afternoon NW flow strengthening to 8–9 kts at surface (8–10 kts at 80m) from 1500–1700 is actually close to ideal for training, though Tomorrow.io flags gusts reaching 11.9 kts by 1700 — worth watching. Afternoon cloud cover going 100% by 1500 limits visibility quality but no precip. Overall the most usable Ed Levin launch for training today, with a good midday window.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Very light 2–3 kts from W/WNW, BL just 70–310m. Calm, suitable for early ground handling and beginner sled rides.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind builds to 5–6 kts NW/W, BL at 340–380m. Best training window — light thermals possible, direction on-face, manageable for P1/P2 under instruction.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW wind 8–9 kts at surface, stronger gusts possible by 1700 (up to 12 kts per Tomorrow.io). 100% cloud cover from 1500. Still flyable for experienced P2 but increasing gust-to-mean ratio warrants caution.

verdict.hazards: Afternoon gusts to 12 kts (Tomorrow.io) punchy for true beginners; 100% overcast by 1500 kills soaring; wind very light in morning

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site powered by the Pacific sea breeze and onshore W/NW flow over the coastal bluff. It needs 10–20 kts from 270–310° for reliable soaring. Marine layer and fog can obscure the site and kill the flying window; KHAF METAR and buoy data are key indicators.
sites.todaySetup: Today's dominant issue at Mussel Rock is the marine layer hanging over the coast — KHAF is reporting mist with 3–6SM visibility and calm winds through 1415Z, and buoy 46012 (Half Moon Bay) shows SST of only 12.6°C with 1.9m/7s swell and 11.7 kts from 330°. The cold SST (12.6°C) and lingering Half Moon Bay mist suggest the marine layer is deeper than usual and burn-off will be delayed, likely not before early afternoon. GFS/HRRR both agree: surface winds at the site are only 3–5 kts W/WNW through midday, far below the 10+ kts needed for reliable ridge soaring. The flow does improve in the afternoon — 7–8 kts at surface by 1600–1700 with 9–10 kts at 80m — which is getting into usable territory, but models show persistent high cloud cover (88–100%) from 1100 onward and Tomorrow.io shows gusts only reaching 5–6 kts. GFS shows a 1% precip probability at the site from 1400–1900, consistent with the cloudy marine environment. The afternoon window is marginal at best; the site never gets the crisp NW flow it needs today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Marine mist present (KHAF 3–6SM BR), surface winds only 3–4 kts W. Not soarable — below minimum ridge-lift threshold. Cloud cover 19–100%.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind builds slightly to 5–6.5 kts W, BL ~305–310m. 88–100% cloud cover, possible light drizzle (1% precip). Below reliable soaring threshold; marine layer influence dominant.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window of the day: 7–8 kts W/WNW at surface, 9–10 kts at 80m by 1600–1700. Cloud mostly clearing to 2–7% by 1600. Marginal soaring possible for experienced ridge pilots, but wind still borderline light.

verdict.hazards: Marine layer mist delays burn-off; wind below soaring threshold most of day; 1% precip risk afternoon; cold SST (12.6°C) sustains deeper marine layer; light wind rotor risk at bluff edge

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland thermal site, W/NW-facing high launch at ~2517ft
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak is a thermal and ridge site that works on valley heating producing thermals and afternoon NW sea breeze. Ideal conditions are 8–15 kts from 280–320° with a well-developed BL. The high launch benefits from stronger gradient flow than Ed Levin and is more exposed to wind shear aloft.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the same inversion problem as Ed Levin — the KOAK sounding inversion at 850mb (1524m MSL) means thermals are capped below the launch elevation of ~2517ft, and BL heights max out at 480m AGL today. Morning winds are northerly 8–9 kts at surface, closely matching the Ed Levin 1750ft data (these sites share similar flow at ridge level), with 80m winds at 9–12 kts and significant wind shear at 120m (1.6–8.8 kts, backing rapidly with height). Tomorrow.io for Mission Peak tells a dramatically different story from HRRR — showing surface winds of only 1–4 kts through the afternoon with gusts up to 10.7 kts by 1900, suggesting very light laminar flow at the actual summit with punchy thermic gusts later. This model disagreement (HRRR 6–8 kts N vs Tomorrow.io 1–2 kts) warrants caution; weighting HRRR, winds are manageable but direction (N/NNE all morning) is not ideal for a W-facing launch. Afternoon cloud cover goes 100% by 1500 killing thermals. The 120m wind layer showing 8.8–10.9 kts from 310–325° by afternoon is notable — at summit height this could mean cross to slightly cross-tailwind component at launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Northerly 8–9 kts surface (HRRR), BL 175–435m. Wind direction off the optimal for W-facing launch; thermals barely developing. Not recommended for launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind lightens to 6–8 kts, backing to NNW. BL peaks ~480m. A brief thermal window possible 1100–1300 if overcast holds off. Tomorrow.io showing very light 1–2 kts at surface — model spread is large.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% cloud cover from 1500 kills thermals. 120m winds clock to WNW 8–10 kts (could mean ridge lift above launch) but overcast negates it. Not a viable flying window.

verdict.hazards: Strong inversion caps thermals below launch altitude; morning wind direction unfavorable (N); significant wind shear aloft above 850mb (34 kts at 700mb); 100% overcast by 1500; large HRRR/Tomorrow.io model spread adds uncertainty

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday looks slightly better thermally — BL heights rise to 380–410m by afternoon, winds lighten to 3–4 kts at midday before NW sea breeze fills in at 7–10 kts by 1400–1600. No cloud cover until late afternoon (100% by 1600). A viable thermal-soaring window around 1200–1500 is plausible, better than today. P3 site.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Similar to Tuesday 1750ft but with more shelter. Midday light winds (2–4 kts), BL 340m by noon. Sea breeze arrives NW 7–10 kts by 1400. Morning start favored; afternoon 100% overcast again by 1600.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Good training day Tuesday — light NW 5–8 kts through midday, BL 310–415m, clear skies until 1600. Best training window 1100–1500 before overcast moves in.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday morning opens calm (1–2 kts, possibly light offshore flow from S/SE at 0800), but by 1200 NW flow builds to 6–8 kts and strengthens to 9–10 kts by 1400–1600. No marine mist forecast (0% cloud until afternoon). This looks like a better day than today — afternoon ridge soaring window around 1200–1700 is promising.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday BL hits 370–415m with clear morning skies. Wind light 4–7 kts NW by midday, good for thermal development. Sea breeze fills in 7–10 kts NW by 1400. Best window 1200–1500 before 100% overcast arrives again by 1600. Better than today but still inversion-limited.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Wednesday (March 25) shows 100% cloud cover all day with BL heights expanding to 490–815m — the inversion breaks somewhat but total overcast negates thermal flying. Winds NW 5–9 kts. Not a viable flying day.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Same story as 1750ft — 100% overcast all day Wednesday, winds NW 5–9 kts, no thermal lift. Ground handling only.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: 100% overcast Wednesday. Light to moderate NW wind. No soaring conditions; possibly useful for ground handling practice only.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Wednesday shows strong NW flow building — 6–7 kts by noon, jumping to 12–14 kts by 1400–1800 at surface with 8–12 kts at 80m. 100% cloud cover all day. Wind speed is finally in the soaring range but the persistent overcast and potentially strong/gusty afternoon flow suggests P3+ only. Rotor risk elevated with stronger flow.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Wednesday 100% overcast, BL 460–700m, winds NW 3–6 kts surface but 80m winds 6–13 kts suggesting shear. Overcast kills any thermal ambitions. Not recommended.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Thursday (March 26) continues 100% overcast with light northerly to NNW winds 4–7 kts. BL 375–800m but no sun penetration. Not flyable for thermals; winds are benign but pointless without lift.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Thursday also 100% overcast with similar wind pattern. No viable flying conditions expected.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Same overcast pattern Thursday. Training hill unusable for meaningful flight without clear skies.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Thursday shows complex wind rotation — morning light/E, building N/NNE to 6–9 kts by midday then backing WNW/NW 6–9 kts by afternoon. 100% overcast all day. Not a classic ridge day; direction changes and overcast make this a no-fly.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Thursday 100% overcast, winds NE/ENE 4–7 kts surface rotating to NW/NNW later. BL 460–735m but fully overcast. No usable conditions expected.

watchlist: Marine layer at Half Moon Bay (KHAF mist, 3–6SM BR, calm winds through 1415Z) — cold SST 12.6°C may sustain coastal fog well into afternoon, delaying Mussel Rock
Strong inversion at 850mb (15.4°C at 1524m MSL) caps all thermal sites — BL not expected to exceed 500m AGL today
Wind shear aloft: 34 kts from 285° at 700mb — pilots reaching ridge height at 1750ft Ed Levin or Mission Peak should be aware of shear layer near summit
Tomorrow.io afternoon gusts 10–15 kts at Ed Levin (all elevations) with light mean winds — punchy thermic conditions developing by 1700 not suitable for P2 pilots
Multi-day overcast pattern: Wednesday–Thursday show persistent 100% cloud cover across all inland sites, limiting flyable days this week to today and Tuesday
tldr: Tuesday is your best bet this week — today is marginal across the board with a strong inversion capping thermals and marine mist keeping Mussel Rock underlit until late afternoon. If you fly today, Ed Levin 300ft has the cleanest window for training (11 AM–2 PM, light NW 5–8 kts), while Mussel Rock might deliver a short ridge session after 3 PM if the coast clears. Wednesday through Thursday is a washout with persistent overcast.
